data.title: Better Home: Sell The Surge With Growth Priced In (Downgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Better Home & Finance Holding Company (NASDAQ:BETR) have been on a remarkable run lately, rallying 67% from a year ago and up more than 3x from their lows. To an extent, shares have been caught up in the “meme” rally that has impacted many housing-related stocks, namely Opendoor (OPEN). I last covered shares of Better in May when I rated the stock a “Hold,” though I saw upside to $20 for investors with high-risk tolerances. Since then, Better has rallied about 150%, an incredible move that certainly would be consistent with a “Buy” rating. Given how much shares have moved and with updated financials, now is a good time to revisit BETR.

Tech investments are driving results

Better Home is a fintech mortgage company, leveraging its technology platform to speed up the timely and costly mortgage origination process. The company is aggressively building out a technology platform, powered by AI, to speed the mortgage process to just one day. In fact, 43% of loans were AI underwritten, and management aims to bring this share to 75%. As you can see below, it is already approving loans more quickly than peers.

Importantly, these loans are also more profitable, given lower operating costs associated with them due to lessened labor needs. We are seeing some signs of improvement, though financial results are mixed. In the company’s second quarter, reported on Aug. 7, Better Home & Finance generated $44 million of revenue, about $5 million below consensus. Encouragingly, AI loans have a ~40% contribution margin, whereas its traditional direct-to-consumer contribution margin is just ~13%.

Growth targets are ambitious with EBITDA still negative

Growing the size and share of AI loans should help lift margins to improve EBITDA. Indeed, management expects AI to grow by ~16% sequentially. This growth is essential if the company is going to deliver on its hope to reach EBITDA breakeven by Q3 2026, as laid out on the earnings call. This is the first time since 2021 management saw a line of sight to breakeven.

Now, Better is still far away from this, and it will need substantial growth just to become a breakeven business, though this is a faster timeline than my 2027 expectation; I am cautious about BETR meeting this target. In Q2, BET reported a $27 million adjusted EBITDA loss. This was actually $4 million worse than a year ago. Now, its exit from its sub-scaled UK operation should provide a benefit in H2. Plus, we are starting to see expenses inflect. They were down 3% sequentially.

Importantly, the platform is growing even in a muted mortgage environment. Funded loan volumes rose by 25% to $1.2 billion. This growth was entirely driven by strength in home equity and refinance volumes, both of which more than doubled. Purchase volume growth was relatively muted at 1% and is still about two-thirds of volumes. Revenue was up a third from last year, outpacing loan growth, thanks to improved margins. Gain on loans rose by 50% to $37 million, reflecting the better margins on its AI-written loans. The company expects total 2025 issuance to exceed 2024 levels.

Now, aside from BETR’s own market share growth, the other potential benefit would be a decline in interest rates, which could increase mortgage activity, both in the purchase and refinance channels. Encouragingly, we are starting to see mortgage rates come down, and they are back near a 52-week low. My view has been that mortgage rates would struggle to sustainably fall below 6.5%, and while this floor had proven resilient, we have pushed below it. It remains to be seen if this decline can be sustained, but if we keep rates below 6.25%, that may help to bring some borrowers into the market. I would emphasize that mortgage rates are much more closely tied to the 10-year treasury than the Fed funds rate. With the 10-year yield having bounced back above 4%, we may see a reversal higher in mortgage rates, and I do not expect mortgage rates to fall below 6%.

Balance sheet action has helped; further equity issuance would support growth

Better has also taken action to improve its balance sheet. During the company’s second quarter, it retired $521 million of convertible debt, adding $210 million of net equity value. The company retired that debt with ~$110 million of cash and a new $155 million debt at ~6%. Essentially, SoftBank, the existing bondholder, took a ~50% loss on its debt position, which speaks to the weakness of Better’s underlying financial position just a few months ago. In hindsight, with the stock having surged so much, SoftBank likely would have been better off holding the convertibles, though such a rally could not have been predicted.

Better now has $77 million of book equity from -$58 million at the start of the year, thanks to this transaction. It has $200 million of debt. BETR has about $575 million in funding capacity. It holds loans for about 26 days, giving it just about $2 billion in total quarterly capacity. This would allow it to grow the pace of transactions substantially from current levels. Another source of value is potentially leveraging its Tinman AI platform and licensing it to other mortgage providers.

Better’s book value is about $5/share, and with its market value now about $500 million, the market is forecasting substantial growth and margin improvement. Ultimately, to justify where shares are trading, we need to see BETR become a ~$50-$60 million EBITDA business at an 8-10x multiple. That will likely require doubling the size of the business, if not growing a bit more than that. With volumes up 25% from a year ago, that would require 3+ years of this growth to get there, an extremely ambitious target. This is a highly aggressive and speculative investment.

I would take profits in BETR

The company would also need to meaningfully increase its warehouse capacity to support larger volumes. With where shares are today, I would rather see BETR issue equity, take advantage of the elevated price to repay debt, further enhance liquidity, and support ongoing investment in technology scaling and refinement. BETR is now trading at a ~60% premium to where I saw upside and is pricing in a material mortgage boom that may not occur given sticky long-term rates.

While meme momentum can certainly persist for some time, this is still a niche, money-losing business that will need to generate substantial scale just to reach breakeven. I am cautious on its ability to hit breakeven in a year, given ~50+% volume growth will be difficult to achieve, especially as larger peers like Rocket (RKT) are also investing in AI and technology to enhance their offerings. As such, I suggest taking advantage of this exuberance under shares to "Sell" BETR at a price that reflects all the good news being priced in, as I still view fair value closer to the $20s.

data.title: Deckers Outdoor: International Expansion And Brand Strength Fuel Upside
data.description:
The chart below is intriguing, right? While the giant NIKE (NKE) is clearly struggling to maintain reasonable growth, smaller brands such as Deckers Outdoor's (NYSE:DECK) Hoka and On Holding's (ONON) On, which I recently started covering, are posting much better numbers.

To explain this, we can point to some mistakes made by Nike. Among them are less innovation in the most recent period and operational execution, such as focusing on DTC at the expense of wholesale execution. But I believe we should also give credit to the other two companies, which have done very well in their execution.

However, despite this positive medium-term trend, DECK stock is facing a more cautious market outlook, which has made its valuation more attractive. As I believe that despite these uncertainties, Deckers remains a good company, my rating is a buy.

Deckers: Two-Headed Growth Monster

As mentioned, I believe that credit must be given to Deckers. Recently, as in Q1 2026, the company has shown impressive growth in sales for its brands, such as 20% for Hoka and almost 19% YoY for UGG, but this has not always been the case.

The UGG brand is now well recognized for its design and comfort, but it was once seen as a fad for the company, since in some quarters, while other brands grew at double-digit rates, UGG saw much worse numbers, including declines in net sales. There may be several explanations for the company's comeback, such as nostalgia among former customers or rebranding to attract new customers, but in the end, the credit goes to execution, since all aspects, such as marketing, repositioning, new products, and even taking advantage of the comfort wear trend, depend on management's vision and the success of the initiatives implemented.

The second and main driver is Hoka, which, along with On, is one of the cases that I find most interesting in this market. Looking at Deckers' older releases (like 2013), the company did not report Hoka revenue, and this is because while the company already had more established brands such as UGG, and Hoka was acquired only in 2012. After that, the company continued to execute well to enhance the brand and reach a wider audience.

Hoka is not that popular in Brazil. In fact, I believe that most Brazilians are not even familiar with the brand yet. The last time I saw someone wearing it in Brazil was a foreign tourist in Rio de Janeiro, but they're aiming to grow in the region as well. This is in line with the global perception that the brand has been gaining, since despite being essentially a sports brand and making running shoes, Hoka is also known for making comfort shoes, such as for tourists and healthcare workers.

This is also the result of years of innovation (although innovation in the most recent period is debatable), marketing, inventory execution, and partnerships with wholesale companies, which have enabled the company to achieve this level of success.

The image below sums up the company well, in my view. Some successful brands in the portfolio, but concentrated on UGG and Hoka, a diversified channel, and an already significant international expansion, but with much more to come. As for the financials, revenue is growing at a pace that I consider very attractive for Deckers' size and industry, and gross margin and operating margin are not only at attractive levels but also growing.

In addition to its history and brands supporting a constructive outlook for growth in the coming years, the company also has some strategies to drive this. For UGG, Deckers plans to “expand product versatility” and increase adoption by male consumers, while for Hoka, it plans to “increase brand awareness through expanded marketing and distribution.” For both brands, Deckers intends to increase market share in some geographies and categories, which seems very possible to me, creating not only optionality for up-selling and cross-selling, but also growth via markets where the company does not have much penetration. As I mentioned, Hoka is little known in Brazil, while in countries such as Chile, the brand has been investing in the community and has had a physical presence since 2024.

Deckers: There Are Short-Term Headwinds

But the reason DECK stock manages to combine an attractive valuation with a compelling story is the uncertainty.

In Q1, the company delivered revenue growth at a rapid pace, but in the breakdown, it is possible to find some red flags. Both UGG and Hoka grew well, but market concerns arise when we look at the geographic breakdown. International grew almost 50% YoY in the quarter, reinforcing that there is still much room for expansion in some regions, while the U.S., which is still Deckers' main market, contracted by almost 3%.

The outlook for Q2 is for revenue between $1.38 billion and $1.42 billion, which at the top of the guidance gives a YoY growth of 8%, much lower than the company had been reporting. In the earnings call, the company commented that there is currently a “choppy U.S. consumer environment,” which is also evidenced by the greater impact on the DTC channel.

In addition to uncertainties regarding softness in the U.S. market, another major uncertainty is tariffs, as the company needs to import its products to sell in the U.S. The company estimates that there will be more than $180 million in unmitigated impact on costs in fiscal year 2026, so even if the company manages to mitigate part of this and is making efforts to do so, there is still a short- and medium-term risk to margins and occasionally to growth as well, as price increases may decrease demand. In other words, the macro environment remains a latent uncertainty for the Deckers thesis.

Although these are relevant risks that deserve to be monitored, I believe they are much more linked to the economic environment than to structural factors within the company. In other words, in the medium and long term, Deckers will be able to resolve these issues through execution and maintain a mix of top-line growth with a healthy margin, allowing for cash flow capable of remunerating shareholders.

Valuation: Priced With Uncertainty

I believe the market is exaggerating the weight of uncertainties somewhat. Again, these are significant risks, but other companies are also facing this challenging environment, and Deckers, in my view, seems to be punished too much considering that its brands continue to have an appealing growth story.

The forward price-to-earnings ratio is 18x. Of the selected peers, On Holding (ONON), Birkenstock (BIRK), Crocs (CROX), PUMA (OTCPK:PUMSY), NIKE (NKE), and adidas (OTCQX:ADDYY), the only one with lower multiples than Deckers is Crocs, and the company faces several uncertainties in addition to tariffs, such as demand for its brand, declining sales, and high debt.

It is worth mentioning that the company has total cash of $1.7 billion, while its total debt is $300 million, i.e., the company has net cash, which explains its better net margins than its more leveraged peers.

When we combine this attractive multiple with long-term estimates, such as the company's ability to maintain double-digit revenue growth for a few years and gain a little margin along with buybacks, causing EPS to grow above 10% in several years, it makes Deckers a potential compounder in my view, and in the long term, it should unlock an interesting earnings yield for shareholders.

Note that even if it does not surprise the market in its estimates, in fiscal year 2030, the earnings yield will already be 8.5% at the current price.

The Bottom Line

Deckers has already proven itself in terms of operational performance through its brands, building a captivating growth story. And although there are some important yellow and red flags that may signal worse prospects in the medium term for the company's sales, I believe that most are cyclical headwinds, meaning that fundamentals should remain intact in the long term.

Therefore, my rating for Deckers is a buy.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Has The Fed Changed Directions?
data.description:
Here is a picture of how the Federal Reserve has managed its portfolio of securities held outright over the past three and one-half years. The last banking week is for September 17, 2025.

Here is what the chart looks like for the past five weeks.

Starting in the banking week ending September 3, 2025, the securities portfolio of the Federal Reserve has modestly risen for a three-week period. This is the first time this has happened in the full period of tightening going back to the middle of March in 2022.

Has a change taken place in the Fed's monetary policy?

Has the Fed moved from a policy of "quantitative tightening" to a policy of "steady as she goes"?

The Fed did reduce its policy rate of interest this past Wednesday, dropping the range of the Federal Funds rate to 4.00% to 4.25% from 4.25% to 4.50%.

Moving from a policy of reducing the amount of securities in the securities portfolio could be considered to be consistent with the drop in the Fed's target range for the Federal Funds rate.

Remember that in the current policy program, the primary policy tool is the size of the Fed's portfolio of securities held outright.

The Federal Open Market Committee may have decided that market conditions required the Fed to stop reducing the size of the securities portfolio if it were going to begin to drop the effective Federal Funds rate.

Right now, financial analysts seem to believe that there will be two more rate reductions this year and one further reduction coming early in 2026.

Ending the period of "quantitative tightening" could be considered to be consistent with this picture of future rate reductions.

But the banking system has lots and lots of free reserves.

Take a look!

The "excess reserves" in the banking system still exceed $3.0 trillion!

So, the commercial banking system is not in any need of "funds" for the short run.

In fact, one could still make a strong argument that the U.S. banking system has excessive cash balances "on hand" and consequently could very easily overwhelm the Federal Reserve system in terms of a growing money stock.

Oh, speaking of growing money stocks...here is what is happening to the U.S. money stock.

The M2 money stock is still growing at a pace that indicates a future rise in inflation. For the past two years, the compound rate of growth of the M2 money stock has been in excess of 7.0 percent.

This should make Chairman Powell and the Federal Reserve Board a little bit on edge.

With Mr. Powell's term as Chair coming to an end, I think this concern needs to get a little more attention.

The monetary resources are already available in the banking system to support a major rise in spending...and bring on a major rise in prices.

Money stock growth is there.

The economy should keep on growing, at least at a modest pace.

The Trump administration is looking for the Fed to do something in the future to boost the economy.

If you look at some of the figures in the banking system, the money is already there. The money just has to be put to better use than it is being handled right now.

This picture, however, seems to point toward more inflation as a coming fact.

data.title: Uniswap Price Slips Below EMAs As Bulls Defend $9.20 Support (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

Uniswap (UNI-USD) is currently trading at $9.36, slipping after failing to sustain momentum above the $9.70–$9.80 resistance zone. The token has dipped below its short-term EMAs, with the 20-day EMA at $9.46, 50-day EMA at $9.49, 100-day EMA at $9.56, and 200-day EMA at $9.71 forming a resistance cluster. Immediate support sits at $9.20–$9.00, while a decisive breakout above $9.80 remains essential to re-establish bullish momentum. The RSI at 43.6 signals weakening momentum, leaning short-term bearish.

Technical and on-chain picture

On-chain flows show a mixed picture. Netflows on September 19 recorded $682K inflows, signaling exchange activity tilted toward short-term profit-taking. Despite this, whales have shown steady accumulation in recent weeks, keeping broader outflows intact. Market cap currently stands at $5.37B, highlighting UNI’s continued leadership among DEX tokens but also its underperformance relative to peers such as PancakeSwap.

Uniswap price dynamics (Source: TradingView)

Technically, UNI has slipped back under the EMA cluster at $9.46–$9.71, keeping near-term momentum capped. Failure to reclaim these levels will sustain bearish pressure if UNI holds the $9.20–$9.00 support. A rebound toward $9.70–$9.80 remains possible, with a breakout targeting $10.40–$10.70. A failure to hold support risks deeper losses toward $8.80.

Fundamental positioning and outlook

Uniswap’s fundamentals remain constructive despite near-term technical weakness. The Q2 financial disclosure confirmed a $110M reserve buffer, securing operating runway until 2027. Additionally, the recently approved $165M ecosystem fund provides resources for liquidity incentives and growth grants. The pending Fee Conversion proposal could redirect $90M+ in monthly protocol fees to UNI holders or reserves, significantly boosting value accrual, though delays in approval have cooled market sentiment.

Rising competition from PancakeSwap and Curve continues to weigh on UNI’s dominance, but Layer-2 adoption and protocol-led scaling strategies keep Uniswap well-positioned in the DeFi landscape.

Short-term outlook

Looking ahead, UNI sits at a make-or-break juncture. Holding above $9.20 support could stabilize price action and set up a retest of $9.70–$9.80, with a breakout opening upside toward $10.40–$10.70. However, a breakdown below $9.00 risks weakness toward $8.80, which last acted as a strong support in August. Broader DeFi sentiment and Layer-2 driven liquidity growth will remain pivotal external drivers.

In earlier analysis, UNI’s repeated rejections at $9.70–$9.80 were highlighted as the key pivot. Current price action confirms this, with UNI slipping back under EMAs and testing critical support zones.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: DXY: Post-FOMC U.S. Dollar Surge Shifts Global Markets (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Elior Manier

A theme that had been building throughout this entire year was how a compromised Federal Reserve independence, combined with a more isolationist US policy (and de-globalization), would send the US dollar (DXY) into shambles.

In fact, this theme has been a favorite for Market enthusiasts, particularly as a compromised US dollar would participate in a rewiring of all financial flows.

Since COVID, a spectacular rise in the USD supply has ramped up inflationary pressures, which got exacerbated by ever-higher government spending, hurting confidence in Fiat currencies.

Particularly after the surprising dovish shift from FED speakers, initiated by Trump-appointed Governor Waller and Bowman, Market participants were afraid of a US central bank that would be pressured by the Trump administration and influenced in its activity, further hurting the Greenback.

This turn accelerated even more after Powell's recent appearance at the Jackson Hole Symposium, which is known for providing market-shambling speeches from central bankers.

It was argued that the speech wasn't as dovish as interpreted, but metals flying higher decided otherwise.

Now, the tides have calmed: the Wednesday press conference, combined with a not-so-dovish 25 bps cut, has proven early dovish speak to be justified, and the US dollar, which had seen catastrophic days leading to the September meeting, is now making a sharp comeback.

Let's examine multi-timeframe comprehensive charts of the Dollar Index (DXY) to see how this change may affect US dollar flows in the long run.

A re-upload of how US dollar movement influences metals

Dollar Index and Metals comparative performance since beginning August, September 19, 2025—Source: TradingView

This chart was uploaded on a piece published yesterday on metals (referenced just above) and is very pertinent to how USD ups-and-downs have a huge influence on trajectories for all asset classes, and particularly commodities.

A Dollar Index (DXY) multi-timeframe comprehensive analysis

Dollar Index daily chart

Dollar Index Daily Chart, September 19, 2025—Source: TradingView

This Daily picture overlook retraces back to how volatile FX and US Dollar flows have been since September 2024.

Between immense buying flows at the end of 2024, followed by an N-shaped downward reversal for the USD throughout 2025, volatility-enthusiasts got exactly what they needed.

You may observe the different themes and dynamics directly on the chart, but one thing to observe is how the most-recent fast-paced fall right ahead of Wednesday's FOMC meeting has been met with a consequent huge rally, with buying flows seeing continuation in today's session.

We'll see more details on this on the short timeframes, but a clear double bottom has taken shape—The rest will be to see how this will influence markets looking forward.

Dollar Index 8H chart and levels

Dollar Index 8H Chart, September 19, 2025—Source: TradingView

Looking closer, we spot how sharp the rebound has been after a huge pre-FOMC descent, which surprised Participants.

Such hedging can occur, particularly ahead of such market-changing events, but the pace and shape of it was one of panic.

The immediate reaction to the dot plot created new 2025 lows, but looking further, an inability of sellers to close below the June lows, supplemented by a switch in the dollar fundamentals, has created another environment for a rebound.

Nonetheless, the buying is currently stalling at the 200-period Moving Average just above the lows of the August range, acting as a momentum pivot.

Moving above the MA would further amplify the upward reversal.

A rejection here, supplemented by a close below the pivot zone (97.25), would send the dollar to another wave of correction.

Levels to watch for the Dollar Index:

Support Levels:

  • 97.25 to 97.60 current pivot, low of August range
  • Major support at the 2025 lows of 96.50 to 97.00
  • 2025 lows 96.20

Resistance Levels:

  • MA 200, immediate resistance 97.90
  • 98.00 August Mid-Range, acting as resistance
  • 98.50 to 98.80 Resistance Zone
  • 100.00 Main resistance zone

Dollar Index 1H chart

Dollar Index 1H Chart, September 19, 2025—Source: TradingView

Looking closer to the 1H timeframe, we see how far and fast the reversal in the Dollar went, bringing the index back above its pivot zone and just above the Pre-FOMC downward trendline.

Buyers will need to hold the retest of that trendline to maintain the path above (located right within the pivot) as overbought conditions put a short-term top to the move.

Now, the rest will be to monitor if sellers to enter here again, invalidating this theme, but momentum doesn't look that way too much—Always keep an open-eye in case a reversal back down happens from here.

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Altisource Portfolio Solutions Stock Is Too Risky
data.description:
Altisource Portfolio Solutions S.A. (NASDAQ:ASPS) has more than doubled this year thanks to improved business performance. Despite its rally this year, the stock remains 90% lower than it was five years ago. Therefore, some investors may think that the stock has ample room to keep rising to catch up with the lost ground of recent years. Indeed, if the company continues to improve its results, its stock will probably remain in relief rally mode.

However, investors should be aware of the high risk of the stock before investing in it. Altisource has a weak business model and a high debt load (more on this later). Its dramatic underperformance vs. the S&P 500 (SP500) over the long run is a testament to the risk of the stock. Highly indebted companies that underperform the S&P 500 over the long run tend to have weak business models, and thus they offer poor long-term returns. Overall, while Altisource is highly volatile and may offer great short-term returns in the event of continued improvement in business results, it is likely to perform poorly over the long run, particularly when compared to the broad market.

Business overview

Altisource is an integrated service provider for the real estate and mortgage industries in the U.S. It offers many services, such as property preservation and inspection, title insurance, real estate valuation, etc.

However, it is critical to realize that the company generates a great portion of its revenue from foreclosure of properties. In other words, while most companies benefit from favorable economic conditions, Altisource benefits from downturns of the housing market. For instance, the stock surged approximately 20% in February 2022, at the onset of the war in Ukraine, which caused a severe global energy crisis and exerted extreme pressure on the financials of numerous households.

Unfortunately for the shareholders, the stock has plunged 91% off its peak in 2022, as foreclosure rates have remained near historical lows. The annual number of foreclosures peaked in 2010, just after the Great Recession, and has steadily declined since then.

As shown in the above slide, mortgage delinquency rates remained near historic lows at 1.2% in May 2025, compared to an all-time low of 1.1% in May 2024. This trend has been exerting a strong headwind to the business of Altisource.

On the bright side (for Altisource), in the first half of this year, the number of foreclosures grew 5.8% over the prior year’s period. Foreclosure starts increased 15% in April and May 2025 over the prior year’s period. As a result, Altisource has improved its business performance in the last two quarters. The company incurred losses per share of -$26.59 in 2022 and $20.09 in 2023 but narrowed its losses per share to -$10.00 in 2024 and only -$1.08 in the last 12 months. After more than 10 consecutive quarters of losses, Altisource posted a profit per share ($1.48) for the first time in the second quarter of this year. This helps explain the aforementioned rally of the stock this year.

On the other hand, despite the 15% increase in foreclosure starts in April and May this year, foreclosure starts remain 29% below the level recorded in 2019. The increase in foreclosures this year has probably resulted, at least in part, from the expiration of a moratorium (protection program) in December 2024. While the uptrend in foreclosures may remain in place for a while, it has proved extremely hard to predict future foreclosure rates.

The depressed level of foreclosures in the last five years has probably resulted, at least in part, from the immense fiscal stimulus packages offered by the government in response to the pandemic. Those packages combined with the Ukrainian crisis caused a surge of inflation to a 40-year high in 2022. While inflation has moderated off its peak, it has remained elevated. High inflation relieves mortgage owners because it results in significant depreciation of the real value of the remaining loan and the monthly payments.

While there are sustained headwinds in the core business of Altisource, its management should be praised for its efforts on diversifying the business model of the company in order to become less sensitive to foreclosure rates.

Altisource has expanded its renovation business from 5 to 22 states. It also recently launched a commercial real estate auction platform. These efforts are obviously in the right direction, but investors need to see consistent earnings in the upcoming quarters to make sure that the company has embarked on a sustainable, profitable trajectory.

As mentioned above, in the most recent quarter, Altisource finally turned a profit, after several consecutive quarters of losses.

The company grew its service revenue by 11% and its business adjusted EBITDA by 10% over the prior year’s quarter. The positive performance partly resulted from the focus of Altisource on growing its renovation business. The company posted earnings per share of $1.48 in the quarter.

Debt

Unfortunately, Altisource has a leveraged balance sheet. Its net debt (as per Buffett’s formula: Net debt = total liabilities – cash – receivables) is standing at $196 million. This amount is approximately 60% higher than the market capitalization of the stock, and hence it is excessive. If the company does not manage to become consistently profitable, its debt will be burdensome.

This is clearly reflected in its income statement, as operating income has not covered net interest expense in each of the last six years; operating income has either been negative or it has been too low to cover net interest expense. Only in the latest quarter did operating income exceed net interest expense ($3.2 million vs. $2.4 million), but more profitable quarters are needed to establish a trend. It is also worth noting that the share count of Altisource has more than tripled over the last three years due to the high debt load of the company.

In addition, the focus of management on EBITDA is a red flag. Warren Buffett has always been critical of managements that focus on EBITDA because this metric excludes interest, taxes and depreciation. Unfortunately, all these costs are real, and hence EBITDA can mislead investors to a great extent. When management focuses on EBITDA, it probably signals that the company is far from becoming consistently profitable. Whenever Altisource shifts its focus from EBITDA to earnings per share, it will send a positive signal regarding the prospects of the company. Until that happens, the stock will have a high degree of uncertainty and risk.

Vast underperformance

The stock of Altisource has dramatically underperformed the S&P 500 over the last decade (-94% vs. +243%) and over the last five years (-90% vs. +100%). In other words, during a period in which the broad stock market has offered exceptional returns, the shareholders of Altisource have incurred devastating losses. This fact should not be undermined by investors. Stocks that underperform the S&P 500 by a wide margin over the long run tend to have a weak business model. As a result, they are likely to continue underperforming the index over the long term. This is especially true for companies with a high amount of debt, which is another signal for a weak business model.

Final thoughts

The sustained losses of Altisource for several years, its weak balance sheet, its focus on EBITDA, and its vast underperformance vs. the S&P 500 are testaments to the risk of this stock. Thanks to improved business momentum and the efforts of management on diversifying the company, the stock has more than doubled this year and may continue offering great returns in the short run. However, this is not a buy-and-hold stock. The investors with a long-term perspective should probably dismiss the stock for the aforementioned reasons.

data.title: StubHub Holdings: Ticket Platform Is On Sale Itself
data.description:
Shares of StubHub Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:STUB) have seen substantial losses coming out of the IPO gate, having fallen over 10% as investors are digesting a rapid slowdown in growth and a very volatile margin profile of the business.

While I am frankly enlightened to see a softer offering in today's IPO market, the discount does not make me attracted to the shares yet, amidst still demanding earnings multiples and concerns about the levels of the fees generated and sustainability of these fees going forward.

Live Entertainment

GrubHub aims to create and be the global destination for live entertainment experiences. Through its marketplace platform, the company has a huge reach, having delivered on more than 40 million tickets being sold, with over a million sellers selling their tickets to buyers across 200 countries, for events held in nearly a hundred countries.

Such events include sporting events, music, festivals, art performances, theater plays, etc., but the one common trait is that these are live events.

The business has been around for a very long time, having been founded back in the year 2000 by Eric Baker, with StubHub focusing on technology-enabled marketplace features to bring liquidity, transparency, and efficiency to an opaque and inefficient second market for such tickets.

While discussions about ticket re-sellers and exuberant profits are well known, the company claims real benefits from its marketplace. Buyers can have greater confidence in buying tickets, knowing that there is liquidity for their tickets in case they are not able to attend an event. The same applies for content right holders, those being teams, performers, etc. Recognizing its potential, the business was bought by eBay (EBAY) back in 2007.

With Mr. Baker leaving StubHub around that time, he ran into the issue that StubHub was not available internationally. He founded viagogo to solve that, with this company ending up acquiring StubHub back in 2019 in a $4 billion deal in order to create a business with a global presence that makes its money collecting fees, commissions, or both, on ticket sales.

Valuation And IPO Thoughts

StubHub aimed to sell 34.0 million shares in a preliminary price range between $22 and $25 per share, with final pricing set at the middle of the range, at $23.50 per share, following solid demand for its offering.

The company obtained gross proceeds from the offering to the tune of $799 million, excluding potential proceeds from the overallotment option.

With nearly 368 million shares outstanding following the offering, the company is awarded an $8.6 billion equity valuation at the offer price. This excludes a net debt position seen around $360 million, and some preferred stock, valuing the business at over $9 billion.

The company has seen solid growth over the past year, as a $1.04 billion business in 2022 reported an operating loss of $217 million. Revenues were up 32% to $1.37 billion in 2023, when the company reported a huge GAAP operating profit of $253 million, aided by strict cost control and notably lower sales & marketing efforts. Revenues were up by nearly 30% again in 2024 to $1.77 billion, as operating profits fell to $138 million, hurt by an acceleration of the cost base again.

Momentum cooled significantly in the first half of the year, with revenues up just over 3% to $828 million, as GAAP operating profits of $52 million were basically at par compared to the year before. However, it seems that pricing of its business model has come down a bit, with gross merchandise sales up 11% to $4.38 billion as the cut of the company fell to 18.9% of underlying ticket sales prices, down from 20.4% this time in 2024. Somewhat worrying also is that second quarter sales fell slightly year-over-year, and while the second half of the year is seasonally stronger, overall results feel quite lumpy in my view.

This valuation discussion above took place ahead of quite some losses seen in the first day of trading, with shares now trading at $20.50 per share. The pullback made that over a billion in value has left the door, with the business now valued around $8 billion, a number equal to about 4.5 times annual sales and, based on the 2024 metrics: nearly 60 times operating profits. Moreover, these profits are lumpy, as all this comes ahead of an uneven performance in the first half of the year.

Concluding Thoughts

Franky, it is somewhat "refreshing" to see not every IPO these days being a runaway success coming out of the gate. The performance of the business has been volatile, notably the margins, as the growth slowdown in the first half of the year is noteworthy, driven by pressure on the "fee" rate.

These fees are still substantial, near 20%, and that can and will invite continued competition. Also, there are risks of technology allowing more direct transactions, as well as platforms being pressured to not facilitate trading in ticks or to avoid massive trading in their tickets. That is for less noble intentions than simply selling tickets if one is unable to attend, but to buy them up en masse in deliberate search for a profit.

Other risks include pandemic, of course, economic changes, changes in Google search algorithms, and the simple fact that profits are very lumpy and modest, even as the business has been operating for a quarter of a century.

Notably, the volatility on the bottom line and lack of substantial earnings here are what make me cautious, as growth has come to a standstill. The modest pullback from the IPO price is not sufficient to entice me to think otherwise.

data.title: Fed Abandons Its New Monetary Policy Framework With 25 Basis Point Rate Cut
data.description:
Fed Cuts Rates By 25 Basis Points

At the conclusion of the FOMC meeting on September 17, the Fed announced that the Committee had lowered the Fed Funds Rate by 25 basis points to a range of 4.00% - 4.25%.

This represents the first rate cut since December 2024, following five consecutive pauses in rates.

The vote was a nearly unanimous 11-1, with the lone dissenter wanting a 50 basis point rate reduction.

The cut was in line with market expectations, and if there was a surprise, it was the cohesion of the Committee consensus, with only one outlier.

The Fed wants to have unity in its decisions as it demonstrates a stable and independent central bank. While cohesion was the first impression, a look beneath the surface showed anything but.

Rationale For The Rate Cut

In the press conference immediately following the FOMC meeting, Chair Powell laid out the Fed’s rationale for the 25 basis point rate cut.

In focusing on achieving the Fed’s dual mandate goals of maximum employment and stable prices, the Fed looks to the balance of risks. With recent downward revisions in job growth, the Fed believes that the downside risks to employment have risen. As for inflation, while “higher tariffs have begun to push up prices in some categories of goods...a reasonable base case is that the effects of inflation will be relatively short-lived—a one-time increase in the price level.”

That being the case, the Fed is now more concerned about the labor market. “We think it is appropriate to reduce rates so that we become more neutral, which will be…better for the labor market.”

Chair Powell Changes His Tune About To The Policy Decision

Over the previous five FOMC meetings, Chair Powell has been patient with regard to making a change to monetary policy.

In April, he said, “We face a highly uncertain outlook with elevated risks to both higher unemployment and higher inflation. The new Administration is in the process of implementing substantial policy changes in four distinct areas: trade, immigration, fiscal policy, and regulation. Our monetary policy stance is well positioned to deal with the risks and uncertainties we face as we gain a better understanding of the policy changes and their likely effects on the economy…. It is now becoming clear that the tariff increases will be significantly larger than expected. The same is likely to be true of the economic effects, which will include higher inflation and slower growth.” (emphasis added)

With the June release of their Summary of Economic Projections (SEP), the Fed made some significant changes in their outlook. The first was they raised their projection for Core PCE at year-end 2025 to 3.1% from 2.8% in March and from 2.5% in December 2024.

The second significant change was to raise the projected unemployment rate at year-end 2025 to 4.5%, up from 4.4% in March and from 4.3% in December 2024.

The Fed was concerned that the projected increases in inflation and unemployment might lead to the dreaded stagflation. Stagflation, by definition, is an upsurge in inflation and sluggish economic growth, characterized by a rise in unemployment. The conundrum for the Fed with regard to stagflation is that the tools to alleviate one problem exacerbate the other. It is a difficult condition to fix.

Yet with these increases in the June SEP, the Fed chose to hold rates steady at that time.

What is curious is that the September SEP leaves the Fed’s projections for year-end 2025 inflation and unemployment unchanged, with Core PCE at 3.1% and Unemployment at 4.5%.

What did change, however, was GDP, which was increased a bit to 1.6% at year-end, up from 1.4% in June.

Additionally, next year’s projections for GDP and Unemployment also improved slightly, to 1.8% and 4.4%, respectively, with GDP increasing by 0.2% and unemployment dropping by 0.1%. Core PCE for 2026 came in a bit worse than June’s SEP, rising 0.2% to 2.6%.

But now, these slightly better employment and GDP projections and slightly worse inflation projections warrant a 25 basis point interest rate cut. Huh?

Chair Powell calls it a “Risk Management Cut” to protect the labor market from further weakness.

Also, the Fed is now projecting a year-end Fed Funds Rate of 3.6%, which implies 2 more 25 basis point cuts by year-end, while in June they were looking for a year-end Fed Funds Rate of 3.9%, which suggested only one more 25 basis point cut.

Abandonment Of The New Monetary Policy Framework

What is even more puzzling is that the Fed completely abandoned their new Monetary Policy Framework that Chair Powell just announced at his August keynote address at the Kansas City Fed’s Jackson Hole Economic Symposium.

The new framework is something Chair Powell has been touting all year as an update to the previous Monetary Policy Framework that was released in 2020.

What is particularly relevant in the new framework is a process for how the Fed should manage Monetary Policy when their dual mandates are in conflict.

It states:

The Committee’s employment and inflation objectives are generally complementary. However, if the Committee judges that the objectives are not complementary, it follows a balanced approach in promoting them, taking into account the extent of departures from its goals and the potentially different time horizons over which employment and inflation are projected to return to levels judged consistent with its mandate.

According to the framework, the Fed should look at how far unemployment and inflation are each from their goals and how long it will take to return to the goals and adjust policy accordingly.

With regard to labor, the Fed looks at the unemployment rate. Despite the large downward revisions to payrolls for July, which so infuriated President Trump he fired the director of the Bureau of Labor Statistics, as well as subsequent weak payroll growth of 22,000 in August, the unemployment rate has only ticked up marginally to 4.3%.

For the prior year the unemployment rate has been in the range of 4.0%–4.2%, which, when Chair Powell has referred to it, he has said, “The unemployment rate remains low, and the labor market is at or near maximum employment.” With the uptick to 4.3%, Powell now says it “remains little changed over the past year at a relatively low level.”

As for inflation, the Fed’s preferred measure is the Core PCE. Since tariffs were announced on Liberation Day (April 2) Core PCE has risen each month to the current 2.9%, which is its highest level in the past year and well above the Fed’s 2.0% target.

In Chair Powell’s words, inflation “remains somewhat elevated relative to our 2 percent longer-run goal.”

So, we have unemployment at a “relatively low level” and inflation “somewhat elevated.” If the Fed’s projections for year-end are realized, the unemployment rate of 4.5% will be 7% above maximum employment, and the Core PCE at 3.1% will be 55% above target. Further, unemployment just moved above target, while Core PCE has been above target for 4 ½ years. And unemployment will get back to target sooner than Core PCE, based on the SEP estimates.

According to the new framework, based on the distance of each from the target and the length of time to return to the levels consistent with the mandate, the Fed should be more concerned with inflation, which would not involve rate cuts.

But they are not. They are more focused on labor.

And, in Chair Powell’s statement on September 17, he only referred to part of the new mandate. He said:

When our goals are in tension like this, our framework calls for us to balance both sides of our dual mandate. With downside risks to employment having increased, the balance of risks has shifted.

He completely ignored and abandoned the part about “taking into account the departures from its goals and the potentially different time horizons over which employment and inflation are projected to return to levels judged consistent with its mandate.”

Why

The question of why the Fed has abandoned the framework they worked so diligently to compose cannot be answered definitively.

We can, however, look to what has changed.

The first change is the composition of the Fed Board of Governors.

With Adriana Kugler’s surprise resignation, effective August 8th, President Trump nominated Stephen Miran as her replacement.

Miran is the Chairman of the White House Council of Economic Advisers. He had a whirlwind confirmation process of just over a month, so he would be in place for the September FOMC meeting. Clearly, the President chose him because he aligns with the President’s craving for lower rates.

During his confirmation hearing, he made a startling statement when he said that if confirmed to the Fed, he would take a leave of absence from his job at the White House. No other Fed Governor has ever remained attached to the White House while serving on the Fed.

While he professed how he would preserve the independence of the Fed during his confirmation testimony, his actions speak louder than words. Miran, who was confirmed by the senate in a 48-47 vote only one day before the FOMC meeting began, was the lone dissenter on the 11-1 vote to cut rates by 25 basis points. He voted for a larger 50 basis point cut.

In addition, his desire for lower rates, like President Trump wants, can be seen in the dot plot for the Fed Funds Rate, below. While there are 19 committee members, only one member is looking for another 125 basis points in cuts by year-end. Although the names are not divulged for each dot, it is reasonable to assume that Miran is the most aggressive voter.

The dot plot shows the wide dispersion of expectations among the committee members, which belies the near unanimity in the earlier 11-1 vote.

One member is looking for 125 basis points in cuts over this year’s remaining 2 FOMC meetings. Six members are looking for no further cuts, 2 members are looking for one more 25 basis point cut, and nine members are looking for 2 more 25 basis point cuts. Showing the true dissension of voters, one member is calling for a rate hike before year's end!

President Trump remains very vocal in his demand for lower rates, asking for a “big” interest rate cut before the FOMC meeting. After the meeting he criticized the 25 basis point cut as insufficient.

President Trump is also pressing to remake the Fed Board in his image. He fired Fed Governor Lisa Cook, although just before the start of the FOMC meeting, a US appeals court rejected President Trump's attempt to oust her. She was able to vote with the majority at the FOMC meeting. On Thursday, President Trump asked the Supreme Court for an emergency order to remove Cook from the Fed board.

In the words of Fed Watcher and Market Strategist Jim Bianco, of Bianco Research, the September FOMC meeting was “a mess.”

He posited: Has the Fed voluntarily given up its independence? Trump is not doing it to the Fed; they are doing it to themselves.

Conclusion

In an 11-1 vote, the Fed cut rates by 25 basis points to the range of 4.00% - 4.25%. This was their first rate cut since December 2024.

The Fed’s rationale for the rate cut was the deterioration in the labor market, as seen by large downward revisions in payroll growth. Although they are also concerned by rising inflation, they are looking through it as a one-time increase due to the tariffs. On balance, they felt the risks to the downside of employment had risen, and they chose to make a “Risk Management Cut” to prevent further deterioration.

In doing so, however, the Fed completely abandoned their Monetary Policy Framework, which they just released in August. According to the new framework:

...if the Committee judges that the objectives are not complementary, it follows a balanced approach in promoting them, taking into account the extent of departures from its goals and the potentially different time horizons over which employment and inflation are projected to return to levels judged consistent with its mandate.

According to the framework, inflation is currently, and projected to be, much further from the Fed’s goals than is unemployment. In addition, based on their projections, inflation will take longer to get back to target than employment. Therefore, inflation should be the Fed’s concern, and cutting rates now is not the solution.

For some reason, though, the Fed is choosing to focus on employment.

Stocks have responded favorably to the Fed’s moves, setting new highs, although the bond market is having the opposite reaction.

Like with the cut last September, although the Fed is cutting short-term rates now, longer-term yields have been rising.

Although on the surface, there seems to be near unanimity in the Fed’s decision, looking more deeply, there is wide variance in the opinion of committee members, and there is no internal consistency in their moves

As Jim Bianco succinctly stated, the September FOMC meeting was “a mess.”

data.title: I'm Raising Cash: 5 Warning Signs Of A Market Bubble Are Here
data.description:
The stock market has been in a clear bull market, and it recently hit new all-time highs. However, there are some signs that not all is well when you take a deeper look at what has been driving this rally and some warning signs that are looming. It is difficult to take your chips off the table, especially when you and almost everyone else is making money. But this is what disciplined investors need to consider doing because I believe the risks of being highly or fully invested in this stock market are growing. Let's take a closer look below at some of these concerns:

The Chart

As the chart below shows, the S&P 500 Index (NYSEARCA:SPY) is trading at or near all-time highs. This 5-year chart shows how far this market has come and that it has experienced a massive rebound from the April stock market lows, which was due to concerns about Liberation Day Tariffs. When I look at this chart, it doesn't look to me like this is a time to invest in the stock market, because the market is overbought and so extended now that a correction could be looming.

1) Stock Ownership Is At Levels Seen Just Before The Great Financial Crisis

According to recent reports, about 62% of U.S. households now own stocks. This might seem like a positive, but historically, this is not the case because when stock ownership is at high levels, it is often a sign of a market top. The last time stock ownership hit the 62% level was back in 2007, just before the Great Financial Crisis. Stock ownership dropped to around 52% of households in 2013 and 2016. The peak for stock ownership was in 2004 at 63%, but just seeing that we are back at levels not seen since just before the Great Financial Crisis is a potentially significant warning sign. Stocks are popular during bull markets and unpopular after investors see losses in bear markets. We are clearly in a bull market and possibly at or near a peak in the popularity of stocks.

2) The Breadth Of This Stock Market Is Not Healthy And Bubble-Like

The market is being mostly carried by just a handful of stocks, and one of the biggest contributors are mega-cap tech stocks. The problem is that many of these companies have announced enormous capex spending plans for AI, and that has created a frenzy for AI-related stocks and tech stocks in general. The total capex spending by the Magnificent Seven is estimated to be around $368 billion in 2025. Without this spending and the subsequent rally in tech stocks, the stock market would not be doing anywhere near as well in 2025. The amount of money being thrown at AI capex seems like an arms race, and the problem with an arms race is that at some point, someone is going to lose, and when they do lose, they will curtail or end their capex spending.

The bulls suggest there is a chance that other sectors (besides tech) will play catch-up and start to show more strength. This would improve the breadth, but the stock market is already at historically high levels, so while this is possible, already rich valuations could limit this possibility. The dot-com bubble experienced narrowing market breadth just before the bubble burst, so the narrow breadth of the market today is a potential warning sign if it does not improve soon.

There is another reason why narrow market breadth is so concerning, and that’s because stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) have ballooned in value, so much so that it now represents about 8% of the S&P 500 Index. This is an unprecedented level of concentration in a major index that has 499 other stocks in it. This concentration risk is huge, and it is even worse because Nvidia reported in Q2 that just 2 customers represented 39% of its revenues for that quarter. With Nvidia being about 8% of the S&P 500 Index, the stock market is highly dependent on Nvidia, and with just 2 customers representing 39% of its quarterly revenues, that means Nvidia is highly dependent on what 2 customers do going forward. If these 2 companies reduce spending with Nvidia, it could seriously impact its revenues, and that could seriously impact the entire stock market since Nvidia is such a huge weighting.

3) Bubble-Like Valuations from Bitcoin To AI Stocks

Bubble-like valuations can be seen in everything from the shares of a major retailer to Bitcoin to AI stocks and more. Extremely high valuations in many areas are often viewed in hindsight (after a major correction or bubble popping) as having been obvious warning signs. But, during bubble-like periods, investors convince themselves that these valuations are the new normal and that this time it is different. It is easy to be convinced that an asset is priced at the right level if others are willing to pay that price. However, we know from history that prices are not always sustainable, and many investors have learned that lesson the hard way.

Costco (COST) is trading for around 50 times earnings estimates.

Palantir (PLTR) is now trading for about 260 times earnings estimates.

Tesla (TSLA) has recently surged and now trades for about 250 times earnings estimates.

4) Speculative Segments That Have Marked The Top In The Past Are Popular Once Again

Special Purpose Acquisition Companies, or "SPACs," are making a comeback in 2025, and we know how that ended the last time these became popular. IPO activity is booming in 2025, to levels not seen in ten years. We know that strong IPO activity occurs during bull markets and peaks at market tops and dries up at market lows. Investors have to ask themselves if they want to be buying at or near a market top when it is fun or buy at market lows when buying stocks is the last thing many investors want to do.

5) Valuation Metrics Are Now At Or Exceeding Past Market Tops

The cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings ratio, or "CAPE," is currently at 37. This is historically high since the CAPE ratio is about 17, on average. Warren Buffett uses a well-known metric called the "Buffett Indicator," which takes the total value of the U.S. stock market and divides it by the Gross Domestic Product, or "GDP." The current estimated value of the U.S. stock market is near $65 trillion, and the U.S. GDP is nearly $30 trillion. This indicates a 216% ratio of market value to GDP, which is unprecedented, and it suggests overvaluation. By looking at the chart below, it is clear that the valuation of the U.S. stock market has surged in the past few years, and it might be at unsustainable levels.

Where I Could Be Wrong

I believe there are clearly many signs of a bubble in the stock market; however, bubbles can get bigger and last longer than many people expect. So, the market could easily rally further, and I plan to benefit if it does, because I am keeping my core positions, but I do believe caution is warranted. As the saying goes, "Don't fight the Fed," so I might be mistaken to sell some stocks into this market rally since the Fed appears to be signaling a new round of easing. Capex spending on AI still appears strong, so if this continues, it could add more fuel to the rally.

In Summary

Asset bubbles have a history of popping sooner or later, and when they do, it can be sudden and often catches many investors off-guard. I believe that even if this bubble-like market has more room to run, it is likely due for a pullback, if not a more significant correction.

I believe being contrarian and being disciplined by raising cash now and waiting for better buying opportunities could be a rewarding strategy that also lets you sleep well at night.

data.title: Invest Smartly: Take A Look At KKR's KKRS And KKRT
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

Today, we present another possible choice with less risk. The focus is on KKR & Co. Inc. (NYSE:KKR) and its baby bonds: (KKRS) and (NYSE:KKRT).

Company Overview

We start, as always, with a few words for the company. So, founded in 1976, KKR is a global investment firm with industry-leading investment experience, with over 3000 employees and approximately 28 offices across 4 continents. The company has a current market capitalization of close to $133 billion. The total assets at the end of the second quarter of 2025 are approximately $380.8 billion. KKR has a BBB+ rating from S&P and an A from Fitch, which puts it in the "investment grade" category.

Below, you can see the company's industry diversification and strategy:

KKR invests in a broad range of sectors, including business services, healthcare, and infrastructure, which reduces concentration risk.

Below, we can see a total return comparison between KKR and SPY, and how KKR outperforms:

3-year total return:

5-year total return:

And here is a 5-year total return with its peers:

The common stock of the company is now traded at $149.34:

KKR has maintained 14 years of continuous dividend payments, the last 5 of which have been in increasing amounts. Here is the dividend history:

The total dividend for the last 12 months is $0.74, and the dividend yield is around 0.5%.

Below, you can observe the forward estimations:

Baby Bonds

KKRT is trading above par, $25.80 currently, with a current yield of 6.67% and a yield to worst of 6.37%. The call date is 06/01/2030 (about 4.7 years away), with a yield to call of 6.37%. The maturity is 39.7 years, with a yield to maturity of 6.85%. It was issued on 05/28/2025 with 22 million shares at a par value of $25 each, generating $550 million in gross proceeds. It is investment grade, rated BBB+ by Fitch and BBB+ by S&P. It pays an interest distribution of 6.88% per annum, or $1.71875. KKRT offers a spread of about 2.1% over the 30-year treasury yield.

KKRS is currently priced at $19, below par, with a current yield of 6.08%. It has close to 0.5 years to call, with a call date of 04/01/2026. The maturity date is on 04/01/2061, with 35.53 years to maturity and 6.46% yield to maturity. It was issued on 03/25/2021 with 20 million shares at a par value of $25 each, generating $500 million in gross proceeds. It is investment grade, rated BBB+ by Fitch and BBB+ by S&P. It pays an interest distribution of 4.63% per annum, or $1.15625, in the form of $0.2890625 per quarter. KKRS offers a spread of about 1.7% over the 30-year treasury yield, which compensates investors for the additional risk.

Disclosure: For those wondering how we calculate the yields, we calculate the internal rate of return for a series of cash flows. We include an initial investment (or current price), periodic payments, and a final payment at maturity, which allows us to calculate XIRR.

Below we can see a comparison between KKRS and its peers in the finance sector, trade below par, and those that have investment-grade credit ratings with their yields to worst:

And here is a comparison between KKRT (at the top) and its peers in the finance sector, traded above par, investment-grade, with their yields to worst:

OTC bonds

The table above shows KKR's OTC bonds with their characteristics. Their yield range is from 4.5% to 5.6%, but they are not rated by the major credit agencies. We prefer stocks that have a higher yield and have investment-grade credit ratings.

Investment Risks

Investing in KKRS and KKRT offers attractive returns but carries risks. Changes in interest rates may affect bond prices. Market volatility in the financial sector may reduce returns. The long maturity and high duration of the bonds expose investors to long-term market risks, and economic instability may affect KKR's ability to maintain stable payments.

Conclusion

KKRS and KKRT offer attractive yields, with an investment-grade rating, making them suitable for conservative investors seeking stable income. Despite the risks of interest rate changes and market volatility, KKR’s strong diversification and $380.8 billion in assets support reliability.
